source.definition: The uppermost floor or space in a building, located directly under the roof.

explanation.sourceText: The English word 'attic' originates from the Latin 'atticus', meaning 'of Attica', referring to a style of architecture from the Attica region in Greece. It came to describe a small upper story or room just below the roof.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „attic” provine din latină, din termenul „atticus”, care înseamnă „din Attica”, referindu-se la un stil arhitectural din regiunea Attica din Grecia. A ajuns să desemneze un etaj mic sau o cameră situată chiar sub acoperiș.

target.definition: Etajul cel mai de sus al unei clădiri, situat direct sub acoperiș.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian word „mansardă” comes from the French „mansarde”, which is derived from the name of the architect François Mansart, who popularized this type of roof.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul românesc „mansardă” provine din francezul „mansarde”, derivat de la numele arhitectului François Mansart, care a popularizat acest tip de acoperiș.

examples.sourceText: The apartment is located in the attic.
It is often warmer in the attic than on the lower floors.
examples.targetText: La mansardă este adesea mai cald decât la etajele inferioare.

examples.sourceText: The attic has slanted walls because of the roof.
examples.targetText: Mansarda are pereți înclinați din cauza acoperișului.

examples.sourceText: Many artists live in the attic because of the light.
examples.targetText: Mulți artiști locuiesc la mansardă datorită luminii.

learningTips.text: Think of the English word 'attic' as the top space in a house where old treasures are stored—just like the Romanian 'mansardă' is the top floor under the roof.
Remember that 'mansardă' comes from the French architect Mansart, who designed special roofs—so 'mansardă' means a stylish attic space.
The word 'attic' sounds like 'attack' without the 'k'—imagine climbing up to the attic to 'attack' your old memories stored there, linking it to 'mansardă'.
Picture a cozy artist’s studio in the attic—this helps connect the English 'attic' and Romanian 'mansardă' as warm, light-filled top-floor spaces.
